coredata.dc:title: Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Fengli
Pei, Haimeng
Kong, Rongmei
Zhu, Shuyun
Xia, Lian
coredata.dc:description: Using sterculia lychnophora seeds as precursors for the first time, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by simple hydrothermal treatment. The quantum yield of as-synthesized CDs was 6.9% by using quinine sulfate as the reference. The fluorescence of CDs could be effectively quenched by a MnO2 nanosheet based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Ascorbic acid (AA) could reduce MnO2 to Mn2+ and result in the destruction of the MnO2 nanosheets, which could induce the fluorescence recovery of the CDs. In particular, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) could bio-catalyze acid 2-phosphate (AAP) hydrolysis to AA. Here, an efficient fluorescence probe based on a CDs-MnO2 nanosheet for rapid and selective detection of ALP was reported for the first time. Excellent performance for the detection of ALP was observed with high sensitivity and a detection limit of 0.4U/L owing to the low background. The detection of ALP in human serum was conducted with satisfactory results, demonstrating its potential applications in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
MnO2 nanosheet
Alkaline phosphatase
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of quantum yield of CDs
Practical analysis in serum samples analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) CDs, (B) MnO2 nanosheets, (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths (excitation wavelengths from 300nm to 420nm). (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CDs, (b) CDs-MnO2 nanosheets, (c) CDs-MnO2-AAP with 200U/L ALP.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black) and the fluorescence emission spectrum (blue) of the CDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets (black) and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs (blue). (C) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with the addition of different MnO2 concentrations. (D) Quenching efficiency. Error bars were obtained from triplicate measurements.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra responses of the biosensor to different concentrations of ALP. (B) The relationship between the fluorescence enhancement and the ALP concentration. The inset showed the responses of the biosensor to ALP at low concentrations. Error bars were estimated from triplicate measurements.
Selectivity of the biosensor for ALP detection. The concentration of ALP and other interference proteins were 20U/L and 200nM, respectively. Error bars were estimated from triplicate measurements.
Schematic illustrations of the sensing mechanism.

head.ce:title: Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, PRChina

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using sterculia lychnophora seeds as precursors for the first time, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by simple hydrothermal treatment. The quantum yield of as-synthesized CDs was 6.9% by using quinine sulfate as the reference. The fluorescence of CDs could be effectively quenched by a MnO2 nanosheet based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Ascorbic acid (AA) could reduce MnO2 to Mn2+ and result in the destruction of the MnO2 nanosheets, which could induce the fluorescence recovery of the CDs. In particular, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) could bio-catalyze acid 2-phosphate (AAP) hydrolysis to AA. Here, an efficient fluorescence probe based on a CDs-MnO2 nanosheet for rapid and selective detection of ALP was reported for the first time. Excellent performance for the detection of ALP was observed with high sensitivity and a detection limit of 0.4U/L owing to the low background. The detection of ALP in human serum was conducted with satisfactory results, demonstrating its potential applications in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), one of the most essential hydrolases that is most effective in an alkaline environment in all body tissues, plays an important role in normal growth [1]. Furthermore, as the most common human alkaline phosphatase, ALP has been identified as an important biomarker in the diagnosis of many diseases. The abnormal level of ALP in serum is closely related to various diseases including breast and prostatic cancer [2], bone disease [3], liver dysfunction and diabetes [4]. The normal level of serum ALP in adults is 46–190U/L, whereas the concentration of ALP in children and pregnant women is greater than 500U/L [5]. Therefore, there is an urgent demand for convenient and sensitive methods for the real-time monitoring of ALP activity. Until now, a number of strategies have been established for the detection of ALP, such as colorimetry [6,7], fluorescence [8–12], electrochemistry [13–16], electrochemiluminescence [17] and surface–enhanced Raman scattering [18]. Among them, fluorescence assays have several advantages, such as high sensitivity, fast analysis, and being nonsample-destructive or less cell-damaging. So far, many fluorescent probes have been established for the detection of ALP activity, such as inorganic semiconductor dots (QDs) [11,19], conjugated polyelectrolytes [8], noble metal nanoclusters [20–22] and organic fluorophores [23–25]. For example, Yu et al. designed a perylene probe for the real-time fluorescence turn-on assay of ALP activity and inhibitor screening [26]. Wang et al. applied the gold nanoclusters for the detection of ALP [27]. Wang group utilized the complexes of conjugated polymers/enzymatic as substrates for the assay of ALP [28]. However, the above-mentioned fluorescent materials or fluorophores are plagued with complex synthesis routes or the involvement of toxic or expensive regents. Accordingly, the development of a simple, economical, and green synthetic strategy for fluorescent materials is highly desired.

The carbon dots (CDs), a new family member of carbon nanomaterials, refers to carbon nanoparticles with a size less than 10nm, which have attracted considerable research interest due to their excellent photostability, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and good aqueous solubility [29,30]. Various methods have been reported for the preparation of fluorescent CDs, such as chemical oxidation methods [31], electrochemical oxidation processes [32], carbonizing organics routes [33] and hydrothermal cutting strategies [34]. Nevertheless, these approaches usually involve complex or post-treatment processes, expensive equipment, or high cost and severe synthetic conditions. Therefore, exploring new methods for the preparation of fluorescent CDs is still desired. In recent years, several researchers have spared efforts on the preparation of fluorescent CDs without the use of organic chemicals in a single step, which is considered as green chemistry. And some green synthetic approaches have been developed for the preparation of CDs by using inexpensive renewable resources as precursors, such as soybeans [35], orange juice [36], gas soot [37], grass [38], watermelon peel [39], pomelo peel [40], ginger [41] and honey [42]. Therefore, a simple, economical and green synthesis of fluorescent CDs is highly desirable. In this work, a simple and green synthesis method for water soluble fluorescent CDs was successfully developed for the first time, using sterculia lychnophora as a carbon source. The advantages of the presented method are mild reaction conditions, simplified operational procedure, good quantum yield and low costs. The quantum yield of as-synthesized CDs achieved was 6.9% by using quinine sulfate as the reference.

MnO2 nanosheets, a kind of ultrathin semiconductor, exhibit a broad and intense absorption band at around 374nm [45,46], making MnO2 nanosheets act as an efficient broad-spectrum quencher. The fluorescence of CDs can be quenched effectively due to the distance between CDs and the MnO2 nanosheets, which is close enough and that fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) consequently happens. Interestingly, it was found that the MnO2-mediated quenching effect can be reversed by AA reduction of MnO2 to Mn2+, leading to the decomposition of the MnO2 nanosheets. On the basis of this finding, it is proposed that CDs-MnO2 nanocomposite be used for the sensitive detection of ALP on the hydrolysis of 2-phosphate (AAP) to form ascorbic acid (AA).

ALP could bio-catalyze AAP hydrolysis to AA, which could reduce MnO2 to Mn2+. The fluorescence of the CDs quenched by the MnO2 nanosheet could be recovered because of the destruction of the MnO2 nanosheets. Using CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites as the probe, a novel fluorescence turn-on approach for the detection of ALP activity was designed for the first time. The presented method exhibited advantages including easy preparation, environmentally friendly and rapid response toward ALP. A high sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.4U/L for the detection of ALP was observed because of the low background. Furthermore, this strategy could also be applied for detecting ALP in human serum samples, which demonstrated its potential for practical diagnostic applications.

Sterculia lychnophora was purchased from a local pharmacy. After its skin was peeled, the sterculia lychnophora seed was removed. L-Ascorbicacid (AA), was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) and reduced N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC. (St. Louis Missour, USA). Glutathione (GSH) (reduced form) and cysteine (Cys) were purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotech Co. Ltd. (Dalian, China). Manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30wt%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). A 10mM Tris-HCl solution containing 0.15M NaCl was used in the experiments (Tris, pH 7.4). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (resistivity >18 MΩ•cm) produced using a Millipore system.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Philips CM200 FEG microscope. UV–vis spectra were recorded using a UV-2501PC spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All fluorescence measurements were carried out on an F-4600 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The instrument settings were chosen as follows: λex=340nm (slit 10nm), λem=420nm (slit 10nm), PMT detector voltage=700V.

CDs were prepared via hydrothermal treatment of the sterculia lychnophora seeds. In a typical synthesis, 1g of sterculia lychnophora seeds was ground into powder and added into 20mL of H2O. Then the mixture was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept heated at 100°C for a period of 1h. The CDs were collected by removing larger particles through filtration with a filtration membrane (0.22µm of pore size). The obtained aqueous suspension of CDs was kept at 4°C for further use.

The single-layer MnO2 nanosheets were synthesized by the previously reported method [43]. In a 50mL round-bottomed flask, 5mL of MnCl2·4H2O aqueous solution (0.3M) and 10mL of tetramethylammonium hydroxide aqueous solution (0.6M) containing 3wt% of H2O2 were mixed within 15s. The resulting mixture was kept at room temperature under vigorous stirring in the open air for 12h. The dried aggregate was separated by filtration (Millipore, type JH, 0.45µm pore size), washed alternatively with Millipore water and methanol several times, and then dried at 50°C. After dispersing in water, the resulting suspension was sonicated for 10h until a brown colloid was formed. Finally, the obtained MnO2 nanosheets with a final concentration of 5mg/mL were stored at 4°C before use.

In a typical reaction, 20μL of MnO2 nanosheets solution at various concentrations was added into 20μL of CDs. The final volume was adjusted to 100μL using Tris-HCl buffer for further detection of fluorescence.

For the detection of ALP, 20μL of MnO2 nanosheets solution (200μg/mL) was added to 20μL of CDs for 15min at 37°C. The AAP solution of 40mM was prepared using Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4). 40μL of prepared AAP solution was mixed with the above CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites. Afterward, 20μL of ALP, at various concentrations, were added into the above-mentioned nanocomposites for 20min at 37°C. The fluorescent signal located at 420nm was recorded.

A novel fluorescence turn-on approach for detection of ALP activity was successfully developed based on the fluorescent CDs synthesized by taking sterculia lychnophora as a carbon source. The sensing process was shown in Scheme 1. The newly prepared MnO2 nanosheets were negatively charged due to the presence of Mn-vacancies on their surfaces, which made them adsorb CDs easily through electrostatic interaction. As soon as CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites were formed, the fluorescence of CDs could be effectively quenched. In particular, ALP could bio-catalyze AAP hydrolysis to AA. AA could reduce MnO2 into Mn2+ and result in the destruction of the MnO2 nanosheets, which could induce the fluorescence recovery of the CDs. The intensity of the fluorescence located at 420nm gradually increased with the increasing addition of ALP. MnO2 nanosheets could effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs and be rapidly destroyed by AA, which ensured the excellent performance of the detection of ALP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of quantum yield of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Quinine sulfate (0.1MH2SO4 as a solvent; QY =0.54) was chosen as a standard. The fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of CDs (in water) were calculated by following methods. Absolute values were calculated according to the following equation:
φ
x
=
φ
s t
(
I
x
/
I
s t
)
×
(
n
x
/
n
s t
)
2
×
(
A
s t
/
A
x
)
where φ is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript ‘st’ refers to standard with known QY and ‘x′ refers to the unknown samples. For aqueous solution, n
x
/n
st =1.

The size and morphology of synthesized CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, the synthesized CDs exhibited good spherical shape and uniform size with an average diameter of 5nm. In addition, the fluorescent CDs were stable and displayed excellent monodispersity in an aqueous solution. Fig. 1B showed the obtained MnO2 nanosheets presented an obvious sheet structure. Fig. S3 showed the obtained CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites. To verify the excitation-dependent emission character of the CDs, the excitation wavelengths were varied from 300nm to 420nm. It was observed that the emission peaks shifted to a longer wavelength and the highest fluorescence intensity occurred at 420nm when the excitation wavelength was 340nm (Fig. 1C). The sensing mechanism of the detection of ALP was shown in Fig. 1D. The synthesized CDs exhibited strong fluorescence emission intensity at 420nm (line a in Fig. 1D), which would effectively be quenched when CDs were adsorbed on the surface of MnO2 nanosheets to form CDs-MnO2 (line b in Fig. 1D). Upon the addition of ALP to the CDs-MnO2-AAP system, the quenched fluorescence of CDs could be obviously recovered with the destruction of MnO2 (line c in Fig. 1D).

UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence spectra were recorded to investigate the optical properties of as-prepared CDs. The CD aqueous solution had a UV absorption peak at 260nm and exhibited strong blue emission at 420nm under the excitation of 340nm ( Fig. 2A). The inset in Fig. 2A showed the photographs of CD solution under white light and a 365-nm UV lamp. Remarkably, the absorbance spectrum exhibited a wide band in the range of 250–600nm with a peak located at 370nm, which was the characteristic absorption of MnO2 and overlaped well with the fluorescence emission of CDs (Fig. 2B). The broad absorbance may make it as the energy acceptor adaptable to fluorescent CDs. To further validate the successful labeling of CDs with MnO2 nanosheets, the as-prepared CDs, MnO2 nanosheets, CDs–MnO2 composite were all analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S1). For CDs, the absorption band at 1656cm−1 could be assigned to the C˭O stretching vibration, indicating the existence of carboxyl groups. The peaks at around 3450, 3260, 2964 and 1245cm−1 were attributed to O–H, N–H, C–H, C–N stretching or bending vibrations, respectively. The results also confirmed that a large number of hydrophilic groups were present on the surface of the CDs, resulting in the good water solubility. The synthesized CDs functioned with hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups may have originated from carbohydrates in the sterculia lychnophora seed. As for the MnO2 nanosheets, the peak located at 515cm−1 could be attributed to the Mn–O stretching vibration. When CDs were adsorbed on the MnO2 nanosheets, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs–MnO2 contained both the main absorption bands of CDs and the MnO2 nanosheets.

Obviously, the concentration of the MnO2 nanosheets has important influences on the fluorescence of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased gradually along with the increasing of the concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets. When the concentration of the MnO2 nanosheets was higher than 40μg/mL, the quenching efficiency was slowed down and then reached equilibrium. The efficiency of fluorescence quenching was up to 96% when the MnO2 nanosheets were 40μg/mL, suggesting that the electrostatic interaction was strong and MnO2 nanosheets were highly efficient nanoquenchers (Fig. 2D). Finally, 40μg/mL MnO2 nanosheets was chosen as the optimal concentration for the subsequent experiments. Detection time was also investigated and the result is shown in Fig. S2. When ALP was added into CDs–MnO2–AAP solution, the fluorescence signal increased rapidly with the increasing of incubation time and then reached a stable stage in 20min. Thus, 20min was chosen as the appropriate time in the following experiments. As Fig. S4 and Table S2 showed, the optimal temperature and time of the syssized CDs were 100°C 1h. Considering the practical application of the proposed system in clinical testing, all the experiments were carried out at 37°C.

To demonstrate the performance of this developed biosensor for detection of ALP, the responses of this assay at different concentrations of ALP under optimal conditions were tested. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence signal of CDs was gradually restored as the concentration of ALP increased, which reached a plateau when the concentration of ALP increased to 1000U/L. The restored fluorescence was dependent on the concentration of ALP. Triplicate tests were conducted for each measurement, and the Standard Deviation (SDs) was shown as error bars. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3B, there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of ALP in the range of 1–100U/L (R=0.983). The linear regression equation is Y=130+8.66C[ALP] (U/L), R2 =0.973. The detection limit was found to be 0.4U/L at S/N=3 on the basis of the *3Sb/slope, *Sb is the standard deviation of the blank samples. In addition, a comparison of the performance of this approach with other reported methods for ALP detection was made and shown in Table S1. The method was more sensitive than most of the recently reported ALP detection methods (Table S1).

The selectivity of the CDs-MnO2 nanocomposite-based fluorescence assay toward ALP was investigated. The selectivity test of the sensing system was then carried out by comparing the fluorescence signal changes of samples containing ALP with that of control proteins, including BSA, HSA, PSA, Human IgG, glucose oxidase (GOx), pyrophosphatase (PPase), Pepsin and Lysozyme. The experimental results were presented in Fig. 4. The ALP solution of 20U/L and interference substances of 200nM were measured using the fluorescent probe, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4, in contrast to the significant increase of the fluorescence intensity induced by ALP, the addition of control proteins triggered negligible changes of fluorescence for the sensing system. These results indicated that the proposed fluorescence sensing system was highly selective for the detection of ALP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical analysis in serum samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability of the sensing system in real human serum samples containing a variety of proteins and other contaminants, the assay for the detection of ALP in serum samples was conducted. The practical application of the designed probe was evaluated by determination of the recovery of spiked ALP in five diluted human serum samples (1%). It is worth mentioning that the interference of GSH and Cys in real samples can be eliminated by adding NEM (0.3mM), a scavenger that can specifically react with GSH and Cys [44].

As shown in Table 1, 1U/L, 10U/L, 50U/L, 100U/L, 200U/L of ALP were added into the serum samples, respectively. The recovery of ALP ranged from 85.4–90.4%, and the RSD ranged from 2.46–3.98%, which were satisfactory for quantitative assays performed in biological samples.

In summary, one-step hydrothermal treatment of sterculia lychnophora seed for green and economical producing the CDs with good water solubility and strong blue luminescence has been proven to be an effective strategy for the first time. This synthetic technique is facile without any complex or post-treatment procedures. The as-prepared CDs and the fabrication process of the CDs-MnO2 based FRET platform were characterized using TEM, UV/vis and FT-IR spectroscopy. Furthermore, the CDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was further used as a novel sensing probe for label-free and sensitive detection of ALP for the first time with the detection limit as low as 0.4U/L. Moreover, the practical application of the probe was validated through the detection of ALP in serum samples with satisfactory results. The present study allows upward scalability in terms of the green production of fluorescent and biocompatible nanocarbons which could be applied in bioimaging and other applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375076, 21205068, 21405094, 21505084), Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province (2015GSF121031), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2013BQ018, ZR2014BM029) and the Project of University-industry Cooperation of Jining City (2014018). The authors acknowledge Mr Marvin B. Jacobson, B. Arch, from Skillman NJ, USA for editorial assistance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2016.11.051.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
